summary: O'Neil shares a personal playlist of songs by male singer-songwriters about their relationships with their mothers. As a mother of three sons, she finds comfort in these songs that explore complex mother-child bonds, noting she'd 'rather have hate than nothing' when it comes to being remembered. The post moves through Sufjan Stevens, Iron and Wine, John Lennon, and Conor Oberst, creating an emotional arc from melancholy to hope.
key_points: Music about mother-child relationships resonates deeply with parents facing eventual separation from their children
Complex or 'fraught' relationships are still preferable to being forgotten entirely
The curated playlist moves deliberately from grief through pain to hope
Art helps process the inevitable letting go that parenting requires
money_quotes: I have a soft spot for sensitive singer songwriting men talking about their mothers, even when it's a fraught relationship.
I've got three sons, and it's nice to imagine they won't forget about me once they leave.
And hey, I'd rather have hate than nothing!
a song I credit with the internal efforts I've made (so far) to let my sons someday leave me
key_insight: The fear of being forgotten by one's children is so primal that even songs about painful mother-child relationships offer comfort to parents.
